description:
You are stepping in as a site-reliability engineer who has just been asked to put a very small “heartbeat” monitoring framework in place for three core services.  
    Your goal is to create the artefacts listed below inside the non-privileged user’s home folder so that an automated system can later pick them up and begin running scheduled checks.

    1. Create a directory strictly at  
       /home/user/sre

    2. Inside that directory create a plain text file named  
       /home/user/sre/services.lst  
       It must contain exactly three lines (one service name per line, **no extra blank lines**):
           nginx
           mysql
           redis

    3. Still inside /home/user/sre create an executable Bourne-shell script named  
       /home/user/sre/check_services.sh  
       The script must satisfy all of the following points (the automated grader will open the file and search for these exact requirements):
         • First line is the canonical she-bang “#!/bin/bash”.  
         • It loads the list of services by reading services.lst.  
         • For every service it uses “pgrep -x” to decide whether a process with that exact name is running.  
         • For each service it writes a single comma-separated line to a log file called uptime.log (see next item) using the format  
           YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS,service_name,STATUS  
           where STATUS is either the literal word “UP” or “DOWN”.  
         • The script must append ( >> ) to the log, not overwrite it.  
         • The path to uptime.log must be hard-coded as “$(dirname "$0")/uptime.log” so that the script works no matter where it is launched from.  
         • Remember to “chmod +x” the script; the tester will verify that the file is executable.

    4. Manually create a starter log at  
       /home/user/sre/uptime.log  
       The file must contain exactly these four lines and **nothing else** (no trailing spaces, no extra blank lines).  
       Line-breaks are Unix “\n”.  
       ----------------------------------------------
       #SERVICE_UPTIME_LOG_V1
       2000-01-01 00:00:00,nginx,DOWN
       2000-01-01 00:00:00,mysql,DOWN
       2000-01-01 00:00:00,redis,DOWN
       ----------------------------------------------

       The header “#SERVICE_UPTIME_LOG_V1” allows downstream tooling to detect the file format version.

    5. When everything is ready, print the single line  
       Setup complete  
       to STDOUT so that the grader knows you are done. (Do not print anything else after that line.)

    Summary of artefacts the grader will look for:
      • /home/user/sre (must exist as a directory)  
      • /home/user/sre/services.lst with exactly the three service names shown, one per line.  
      • /home/user/sre/check_services.sh that is executable and contains the required logic.  
      • /home/user/sre/uptime.log with the exact four lines shown above.  
      • Final console output: “Setup complete”

    You do NOT need to actually run the monitoring script; just create the files with the correct contents and permissions.
truth:
The following items will be verified:

    1. Directory existence:
       • Path: /home/user/sre  
         – Must be type “directory”.

    2. /home/user/sre/services.lst:
       • Exactly three lines, in order:
         nginx
         mysql
         redis

    3. /home/user/sre/check_services.sh:
       • Must exist.
       • Must have Unix executable bit set for the user (chmod 755 or similar).
       • File must start with: #!/bin/bash
       • Somewhere in the file must appear the literal text: pgrep -x
       • Somewhere in the file must append to uptime.log using “>>”.
       • Must reference path with $(dirname "$0")/uptime.log (string comparison).

    4. /home/user/sre/uptime.log:
       • Must exist.
       • Exact content (four lines, newline-delimited):
         #SERVICE_UPTIME_LOG_V1
         2000-01-01 00:00:00,nginx,DOWN
         2000-01-01 00:00:00,mysql,DOWN
         2000-01-01 00:00:00,redis,DOWN

    5. Terminal output:
       • After all commands, the last line printed to STDOUT must be literally:
         Setup complete
name: task_000000_8254645f
